seo.title: Plausible vs Fathom vs Umami: Privacy-First Analytics Compared
seo.description: Compare Plausible, Fathom, and Umami for privacy-first web analytics. Features, pricing, GDPR compliance, and self-hosting compared.

content.intro: Choosing a privacy-first analytics tool is no longer optional for EU-facing SaaS products — it's a GDPR necessity. Google Analytics requires consent banners for most EU use cases. Plausible, Fathom, and Umami offer cookie-free, GDPR-compliant alternatives that work without consent banners in most cases. Here's how they compare on every dimension that matters.

options.name: Plausible
options.best_for: Most SaaS teams starting out with privacy-first analytics

options.name: Fathom
options.best_for: Teams that want zero configuration and strong EU data residency

options.name: Umami
options.best_for: Self-hosters who want full data control at minimal cost

criteria.name: GDPR Compliance (no consent banner)
criteria.description: Whether the tool can be used without a cookie consent banner for EU visitors

values.Plausible: Yes — no cookies, no personal data storage, no consent required in most cases
values.Fathom: Yes — cookie-free, no personal data, consent-free for most EU use
values.Umami: Yes — no cookies by default, but depends on self-host configuration

criteria.name: Pricing
criteria.description: Monthly cost for typical SaaS usage (up to 100k pageviews/month)

values.Plausible: $9/month cloud (up to 10k pageviews) — $19 for 100k; free to self-host
values.Umami: Free to self-host; cloud starts at $9/month

criteria.name: EU Data Residency
criteria.description: Whether data is stored in the EU by default

values.Plausible: Yes — EU-based hosting by default on cloud plan
values.Fathom: Yes — EU isolation option available on higher plans
values.Umami: Depends on where you self-host; cloud is US-based

criteria.name: Script Size
criteria.description: Size of the analytics tracking script (affects page performance)

criteria.name: Self-Hosting
criteria.description: Ease and quality of self-hosting option

values.Plausible: Yes — Docker image, well-documented, community support
values.Umami: Yes — primary deployment model, excellent Docker support

criteria.name: Real-Time Data
criteria.description: Whether real-time visitor data is available in the dashboard

criteria.name: Custom Events
criteria.description: Support for tracking custom user interactions beyond pageviews

values.Plausible: Yes — custom events and goals with a simple API
values.Fathom: Yes — event tracking with custom attributes
values.Umami: Yes — custom events with full flexibility on self-hosted

criteria.name: Revenue Tracking
criteria.description: Ability to track revenue events and conversion value

values.Plausible: Yes — revenue goals with monetary values
values.Umami: Not natively — requires custom event workarounds

criteria.name: Team & Multi-Site Support
criteria.description: Managing multiple sites and team member access

values.Plausible: Yes — multi-site dashboard, team members with roles
values.Fathom: Yes — unlimited sites on all plans, team sharing

criteria.name: Open Source
criteria.description: Whether the codebase is publicly available and auditable

verdict.summary: All three tools are strong GDPR-compliant alternatives to Google Analytics. Plausible hits the best balance of features, EU hosting, and ease of use for most SaaS teams. Fathom wins on simplicity — zero config, reliable, and great support. Umami is the choice when budget is a constraint and you're comfortable self-hosting.

use_cases.scenario: EU-based SaaS startup, no DevOps resource
use_cases.reason: EU hosting by default, easy setup, no infrastructure to manage, compliant out of the box.

use_cases.scenario: Developer who wants zero ongoing maintenance
use_cases.reason: Cloud-only means no servers to maintain, excellent uptime, and strong support.
Free, full data ownership, can host on the same VPS as your app.

use_cases.scenario: Tracking revenue and conversions alongside traffic
use_cases.reason: Both have native revenue tracking; Umami requires workarounds for monetary event values.
